quoted_tweet.text:
At one point Ohio Gubernatorial candidate Casey Putsch was exploding in popularity and was quickly gaining traction. 

Then, all of the "America First" influencers and commentors seemed to leave him hanging, and the social media platforms worked hard to censor him and suppress him.

He got no visibility, no word-of-mouth, and no support. You can say he "ran a weak campaign" all you want, but this is by design. The establishment wants it to be impossible to run on what Casey ran on. At the very least they want it to SEEM impossible.

If we want America First candidates, we need to be prepared to heavily support them, advertise for them, protect them, and work to fight against the establishments efforts to silence them.

No one ever said it would be easy to make America First happen. It will be incredibly difficult even. Are we ready to actually put effort into this movement?

kept.text:
Would be keen to understand how you computed the volume figures. From what I’m seeing, @Polymarket still maintains a slight lead, both when measuring volume in terms of shares traded and dollar volume.

That said, the broader trend remains intact. @Kalshi has captured a meaningful share of Polymarket’s crypto market volume since the start of the year, particularly as activity around short-duration markets has accelerated.

Digging deeper into Kalshi’s activity, the volume composition trend looks increasingly similar to Polymarket’s. 

Short-duration 15-minute and hourly markets now dominate trading activity, reinforcing the idea that the real growth engine in PMs is shifting toward higher-frequency, volatility-driven products.

There is $240 billion trapped behind a bad user experience, and we’re about to unlock it.

Bernstein says we're going from $50 billion to $240 billion this year alone.

Robinhood just made $435,000,000 from a category that is fundamentally BROKEN. They even called it the fastest-growing business in their history.

Prediction markets are on track to becoming a trillion-dollar category, but here is the number that matters most:

Kalshi said 70% of their users don't even trade. That means money doesn’t move because the interface is failing the user.

We recognize the challenge at @Yellow, and we're partnering with builders like @VizoExchange who are rethinking the entire experience. They're building something I think is truly unique. Genuinely different. Instead of forcing you to search through live markets, Vizo puts a news feed first.

If you're a builder in Miami, I'd love to catch up and explore the prediction market opportunity together.

Say hello!

kept.text:
one of the reasons i never promoted polymarket or kalshi (apart from the fact that they offered very little money) is the fact that you can never beat someone with an insider information.

- some openai employee will know when the next gpt model drops
- some apple employee will know when the next iphone drops
- some disney employee will know when the next movie releases

so when you place your "bets", you're essentially funding liquidity for someone who knows that information already.

"prediction markets" are just gambling casinos in your pocket. you shouldn’t really play with them unless you know the outcome already

profile_bio.description: Co-chair of Arnold Ventures. Reality is more nuanced than the headline.

kept.text:
Recent crypto VC funding just hit staggering close to $5.64B+ and the majority is coming from these:

- @a16zcrypto $2.2B
- @HaunVentures $1B
- @dragonfly_xyz $650M

Top-tier firms like these are still raising/deploying big because LPs see durable adoption in several categories like:

- Stablecoins + payment infra (~$300B+ supply, real revenue),
- RWAs/tokenization
- Prediction markets
- AI agents needing on-chain rails.

This is also reflected in the latest funding round for each VCs above:

➠ a16zcrypto
- @Kalshi ~$1B in Mar '26
- @bettermoney_co ~$10M in Mar '26
- @KairosTradeX ~$2.5M in Feb '26
- @babylonlabs_io ~$15M in Jan '26
- @jito_sol ~$50M in Oct '25 (strategic)

➠ Haun
- @ereborbank ~$350M in Dec '25 + Feb '26
- @SquadsLabs  ~$18M in Apr '26 (strategic)

➠ Dragonfly
- @raincards $250M Series C in Jan '26
- @Polymarket $1B+ in 2026
- @ethena Multiple key investment across 2025 est. $100M+

Market is cooling. From Q1 through April 2026, funding is down sharply. Mega-rounds are scarcer. Sorry to break your hopium: these firms aren’t lining up to buy and save your bags. Most of the funds will be used for growth with some exceptions.

What they are buying is the part of the cycle where infrastructure becomes product: stablecoin rails, on-chain credit, tokenized securities, agentic payment systems.

Categories where usage compounds, not attention.

If you’re positioning, position with that thesis, and consider expanding into adjacent verticals that VCs still haven’t touched, but correlate.

NFA. DYOR

kept.text:
Kalshi crossing 50% isn’t just “growth”, it’s the first real sign that Polymarket might be losing its grip on the narrative.

Feels like the space is getting more competitive right as broader market conditions are starting to stabilize a bit… which usually brings more attention (and volume) back to these kinds of platforms.

Last 4 weeks volume paints a clearer picture:

➜ Kalshi – $13.4B (52%)
➜ Polymarket – $8.5B (33%)
➜ Limitless – $1.4B (5%)
➜ https://t.co/B6qxyQN931 – $1.3B (5%)
➜ Opinion Labs – $636M (qqq13%)

A few things stand out on a closer look:

Kalshi has quietly taken control of the market here… holding >50% share consistently through April and gradually eating into Polymarket’s dominance.

Not something that happened overnight either, looks more like steady accumulation of users + liquidity.

Also worth noting the fight for 3rd place is starting to heat up.

Limitless pushing $1.4B over the last 4 weeks (up ~177% from the previous period) and breaking into 3rd for the first time this year.

That kind of jump usually doesn’t go unnoticed… especially in a market where attention tends to rotate quickly.

Another signal:

April 20–26 hit a new weekly high of $7.1B in volume.

Suggests participation is picking up again, not just shifting between platforms.

Overall, feels like:

◈ Market conditions stabilizing → more activity
◈ Clear leader emerging (for now)
◈ Mid-tier platforms getting more aggressive

Still early, but definitely turning into a more competitive landscape than it was a few months ago.

profile_bio.description: #Defi from GEN-Z • | Follow me for On-Chain ⛓️ Strategies & early gems 🧠 | Ambassador: @AstraAIofficial

kept.text:
A Telegram bot trading Polymarket or Kalshi runs someone else's strategy on your money.

You hand over signing rights. The operator picks the trades. The chat says "position opened" and "position closed" with no rule you can read back. When it loses, you have a notification and a smaller wallet.

On @ElasticsAI, you write the rule. IF probability on a tagged contract crosses X% → THEN sell Y% (your choice). The LLM assembles that workflow from pre-coded modules — it does not decide what to trade. Execution is deterministic. Only execution modules touch markets, only within the limits you set.

Every trigger and fill is logged and replayable. If a position closes, you can read the IF→THEN that fired. When the capital is yours, that is non-negotiable.

kept.text:
Who is Luana Lopes Lara? 

> She grew up in a steady Brazilian household where education was the default setting.  

> Her dad worked as an engineer.  
> Her mom taught math.  
> The kind of environment where being curious wasn’t optional.  

> Ballet came early.  
> Eight years at the Bolshoi school.  
> Long days, strict routines, and a level of discipline most people never experience.  

> It didn’t turn her into a performer.  
> It turned her into someone who can handle pressure without blinking.  

> Academics followed the same pattern.  
> Astronomy Olympiad gold.  
> Math Olympiad bronze.  
> A quiet track record of doing hard things well.  

> MIT at eighteen.  
> Computer science and math.  
> Internships at Bridgewater and Citadel that showed her how the top of finance actually works.  

> Then she met Tarek.  
> Two people looking at the same idea:  
> If events move markets, why can’t events be markets.  

> That became Kalshi.  
> A prediction exchange built inside one of the most regulated corners of finance.  

> It took years of filings, meetings, and waiting.  
> Most startups don’t survive that timeline.  
> They did.  

> Eventually the approval came.  
> The exchange launched.  
> The idea worked.  

> By 2025, Kalshi hit an eleven‑billion valuation.
 
> She became the youngest self‑made woman billionaire.  

> People call it a breakthrough.  
> It looks more like consistency.
